title: App Modularization & Build System
description: Module types, dependency graphs, version catalogs, convention plugins, KSP, and build optimization

references.title: Android Developers — Guide to App Modularization

lesson:
Why Modularize?

Modularization is the #1 differentiator at senior Android interviews. A well-modularized app has faster builds (parallel compilation), stricter encapsulation, independent feature teams, and reusable components.

Module Types (Now in Android Pattern)

:app ├── :feature:home ├── :feature:search ├── :feature:profile │ ├── :core:data (repositories, data sources) ├── :core:network (Retrofit, OkHttp setup) ├── :core:database (Room DAOs, entities) ├── :core:ui (shared composables, theme) ├── :core:model (domain models, no Android deps) ├── :core:common (utilities, extensions) └── :core:testing (test utilities, fakes)
Key rule: Feature modules NEVER depend on each other. They only depend on :core:* modules. This prevents circular dependencies and enables parallel builds.

api vs implementation

Gradle dependency configurations control visibility and build speed:

ConfigurationBehaviorUse When
implementationDependency is internal — consumers can't see itDefault choice. Keeps build fast.
apiDependency is exposed — consumers can use it directlyOnly when your public API exposes types from the dependency
// :core:data/build.gradle.kts dependencies { implementation(libs.retrofit) // internal — feature modules can't see Retrofit types api(libs.kotlinx.coroutines.core) // exposed — features use Flow/coroutines from this api(project(":core:model")) // exposed — features use domain models }
Why this matters: With implementation, changing Retrofit doesn't recompile feature modules. With api, it would. Prefer implementation unless the type appears in your public API.

Version Catalogs

Centralize all dependency versions in gradle/libs.versions.toml:

# gradle/libs.versions.toml [versions] kotlin = "2.0.0" compose-bom = "2024.06.00" retrofit = "2.11.0" room = "2.6.1" [libraries] retrofit-core = { group = "com.squareup.retrofit2", name = "retrofit", version.ref = "retrofit" } room-runtime = { group = "androidx.room", name = "room-runtime", version.ref = "room" } room-compiler = { group = "androidx.room", name = "room-compiler", version.ref = "room" } [bundles] room = ["room-runtime"] [plugins] kotlin-android = { id = "org.jetbrains.kotlin.android", version.ref = "kotlin" }

Then in any module: implementation(libs.retrofit.core) — type-safe, auto-completed by IDE.

Convention Plugins

Instead of copy-pasting build config across 20 modules, define reusable plugins in build-logic/:

// build-logic/convention/src/main/kotlin/AndroidFeatureConventionPlugin.kt class AndroidFeatureConventionPlugin : Plugin<Project> { override fun apply(target: Project) { with(target) { pluginManager.apply("com.android.library") pluginManager.apply("org.jetbrains.kotlin.android") extensions.configure<LibraryExtension> { compileSdk = 35 defaultConfig.minSdk = 26 } } } } // :feature:home/build.gradle.kts plugins { id("myapp.android.feature") // One line instead of 30! }

KSP vs KAPT

KAPTKSP
Uses Java annotation processing via stubsKotlin-native symbol processing
Generates Java stubs first → slowOperates on Kotlin directly → up to 2x faster
Legacy: Room, Dagger/Hilt (migrating)Room, Moshi, and most modern libraries
// Migration: replace kapt with ksp plugins { id("com.google.devtools.ksp") version "2.0.0-1.0.22" } dependencies { ksp(libs.room.compiler) // ✅ Fast // kapt(libs.room.compiler) // ❌ Slow — remove this }

Build Performance

  • Configuration cache — Caches the build configuration phase: org.gradle.configuration-cache=true
  • Parallel execution — Build independent modules simultaneously: org.gradle.parallel=true
  • Build cache — Reuse outputs from previous builds: org.gradle.caching=true

R8 Full Mode vs Compat Mode

R8 full mode enables more aggressive optimizations:

// gradle.properties android.enableR8.fullMode=true

Full mode: removes more unused code, may break if you rely on implicit keep rules (e.g., reflection). Always test after enabling. Compat mode is the safe default that follows ProGuard behavior.

questions.title: Module Dependency Rules
questions.description: In a properly modularized Android app following the Now in Android (NIA) architecture, which dependency rule is correct?
options: Feature modules can depend on other feature modules for code sharing
Feature modules depend only on :core:* modules, never on each other
Core modules can depend on feature modules for UI components
:app depends only on :core:data, features register themselves
questions.explanation: Feature modules must NEVER depend on each other — this would create tight coupling and slow builds. Features depend only on :core:* modules. The :app module depends on all features to assemble them. Core modules never depend on features. This DAG (directed acyclic graph) enables parallel compilation.
hints: What would happen to build parallelism if :feature:home depended on :feature:profile?

questions.title: api vs implementation
questions.description: If :core:data uses implementation(libs.retrofit), what happens when a :feature:home module depends on :core:data?
options: :feature:home can use Retrofit classes directly
:feature:home cannot see Retrofit classes — they are internal to :core:data
The build fails because Retrofit is not available
:feature:home gets Retrofit but only at runtime, not compile time
questions.explanation: implementation means the dependency is internal. :feature:home can use :core:data's public API but cannot access Retrofit types. This is encapsulation — if :core:data switches from Retrofit to Ktor, no feature modules need to change. Use api only when your module's public interface exposes types from the dependency.
hints: implementation = private, api = public in terms of transitive visibility

questions.title: Version Catalog Structure
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to complete this version catalog entry in libs.versions.toml.
questions.explanation: Version catalogs have four sections: [versions] (version numbers), [libraries] (Maven coordinates), [bundles] (groups of libraries), and [plugins] (Gradle plugins). version.ref links a library to a version, ensuring consistency.
hints: The TOML file has sections in square brackets
version.ref points to a key in the versions section

questions.title: Convention Plugin
questions.description: What does this convention plugin achieve?
options: It creates a new Android feature module from scratch at runtime
It applies a standard set of plugins, SDK versions, Compose support, and shared dependencies so every feature module has consistent configuration with one line
It enforces that feature modules cannot depend on each other by analyzing the dependency graph
It generates build variants for debug and release configurations
questions.explanation: Convention plugins centralize build configuration. Instead of repeating compileSdk, minSdk, Compose setup, Hilt, and common dependencies in every build.gradle.kts, this plugin applies them all with id("myapp.android.feature"). This is the build-logic/ pattern used by Now in Android — it reduces duplication and ensures consistency across all feature modules.
hints: Think DRY principle applied to build configuration
The Now in Android project uses this pattern in build-logic/

questions.title: KSP vs KAPT
questions.description: Why is KSP (Kotlin Symbol Processing) faster than KAPT?
options: KSP generates less code than KAPT
KSP operates directly on Kotlin symbols without generating intermediate Java stubs
KSP runs at runtime instead of compile time
KSP uses incremental processing while KAPT processes everything from scratch
questions.explanation: KAPT first generates Java stubs from Kotlin code, then runs Java annotation processing on those stubs — a costly extra step. KSP is Kotlin-native and processes Kotlin symbols directly, skipping stub generation entirely. This makes it up to 2x faster. Most modern libraries (Room, Moshi) now support KSP.
hints: The bottleneck in KAPT is an intermediate conversion step

questions.title: Leaking Internal Dependencies
questions.description: This module's build configuration leaks implementation details. Feature modules are now tightly coupled to Retrofit. Fix it.
questions.explanation: Retrofit, Moshi converter, and OkHttp logging are implementation details of :core:network. Feature modules should call repository.getUsers() — they don't need to know it uses Retrofit underneath. Only :core:model should be api because data classes from it appear in the module's public function signatures.
hints: Ask: do feature modules need to directly use Retrofit classes?
Which dependency's types appear in the module's public API?

questions.title: Inter-Module Navigation
questions.description: Feature modules can't depend on each other, so how do you navigate from :feature:home to :feature:profile? Write a shared navigation route definition in :core:navigation and the navigation graph setup.

checks.message: Define type-safe routes in the shared module
Show navigation using the shared routes

questions.explanation: The pattern: (1) Define routes in :core:navigation which all modules can access. (2) Feature screens expose navigation events as lambda callbacks (e.g., onNavigateToProfile). (3) The :app module wires everything together in NavHost, since it depends on all features. This way :feature:home never imports anything from :feature:profile.
hints: The :app module is the only one that depends on all feature modules
Feature screens should expose navigation events as callbacks, not perform navigation directly

questions.title: Build Performance
questions.description: Which Gradle property enables reusing task outputs from previous builds, significantly speeding up incremental builds?
options: org.gradle.parallel=true
org.gradle.caching=true
org.gradle.configuration-cache=true
org.gradle.daemon=true
questions.explanation: org.gradle.caching=true enables the build cache, which stores and reuses task outputs. If inputs haven't changed, the cached output is used instead of re-running the task. parallel runs independent modules simultaneously. configuration-cache caches the build configuration phase. All three should be enabled for optimal build speed.
hints: Which setting is specifically about reusing previous outputs?

questions.title: R8 Full Mode
questions.description: What is the key difference between R8 full mode and R8 compat mode?
options: Full mode only works on Kotlin code, compat mode handles Java
Full mode does not obfuscate, compat mode does
Full mode applies more aggressive optimizations and may remove classes kept implicitly by ProGuard rules — requires explicit keep rules for reflection
Full mode produces larger APKs with better runtime performance
questions.explanation: R8 compat mode follows ProGuard semantics — it keeps classes that ProGuard would keep implicitly. Full mode is more aggressive: it may remove classes only accessed via reflection unless you add explicit -keep rules. This produces smaller APKs but requires testing. Enable with android.enableR8.fullMode=true.
hints: Full mode breaks implicit assumptions about which classes are kept

questions.title: Build Variants
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to define product flavors for a free and paid version of the app.

blanks.hint: The block that defines app variants
Unique identifier for each variant on the Play Store

questions.explanation: productFlavors create variants of your app with different configurations. Each flavor can have its own applicationId (allowing both free and paid versions on the same device), buildConfigField values, resources, and source sets. Combined with build types (debug/release), they produce build variants like freeDebug, paidRelease.
hints: Product flavors let you build multiple versions of the same app
Each variant needs a unique package identifier to coexist on a device
